## [03:00 - 06:01]

I know how to read people. I know how to size them up. I know how to get people talking to me and I know how to evaluate the critical data. I know how not to be taken advantage of. And if you try to take advantage of me, I know how to turn it and take advantage of that situation. Of course, you know, you can't take anything at true face value until you see the actual data. Finish your due diligence regardless of what your broker tells you. Ask your broker, what's the downside here? What downside do you see? If I spend this money fixing the roof, the poly, putting on a new clubhouse, what's the downside of doing it? By the way, what's the downside if I don't do it? After you've asked about the good, ask about the bad. What's the upside? What's the downside? Ask both sides and don't move off until you get an answer. If the broker gives you any area to watch or look at or pay attention to, then pivot back to the positives. Reel them back in with a conversation so you get good due diligence from the professional, the partner that you brought in to represent you as a buyer or that's representing the seller and trying to put the two together. Look if they stall or say nothing else, negative, ask specific questions. If they stall, that means something. If they don't answer, that means something. If they answer too excitedly, that means something. Learn to size people up. If you don't know how to do this, grab my programs from Cardone University. Now, ask yourself, what's the downside of this deal? What's the worst case scenario? If you're going to find something wrong with this deal, what might it be? You're asking these questions. ## [06:01 - 09:03]

It's part of the due diligence. And it's public data, bro. Come on, man. I don't care what they paid. I just want to know. I don't care how much money they're making on me. I just want to know. And by the way, you deserve to know. Your investors deserve to know. And I guarantee you the bank knows what it traded for last time. Let's not skit ourselves. They should know. Or, if not, they have to be able to get that data. By the way, trading questions, when they traded last, when it was bought last, segues perfectly into the next question, which this is a mastery part of my buying and how I do deals, which focuses on another big expense here. Are you adjusting for new taxes in your pro forma? So this question, by the way, would segue you perfectly into your pro forma that the broker provided you. It's there. You're going to already know it, but you're going to ask them, are you adjusting for new taxes in your pro forma? Meaning, when you buy the property from them and they bought it, assumingly, for less than you did, there's going to be higher property taxes. So you're going to ask, are you adjusting for new taxes? Again, part of your due diligence. This is a big question. But at the same time, a pro forma means almost nothing. It's speculation of the future, most of which was made up by a broker, most of which assumes the best on the rental income and the lowest on the expenses. As a side note, by the way, you can expect expenses to grow by 3% while revenue will only grow by 2%. But don't be alarmed. Even though your expenses are growing at 3% a year and your revenue is only growing by 2% a year, remember, your revenue should be double, at least double what your expenses are anyway. Look, pro forma means future. It's hoping for the best. It's not a lie. It's just made up. Nobody knows what the future is in a year or two years. Ten years out, you can pretty much assume what that neighborhood's going to be like. It's a much easier to predict long term. Short of some nuclear or major earthquake or biological or internet technology failure, there's a good chance ten years from now, if you're picking the right neighborhood, this property will have higher rent. That means it'll appraise for more money, which means you can sell it for a profit. Use the data to guide you forward with certainty. Don't guess. That said, the pro forma does show you existing taxes on the property and should show you the increase. Compare the pro forma that they provided with what you believe the property will require. Also compare those two things to the metrics of the marketplace. Know the metrics of the marketplace when it comes to insurance, rent, concessions, vacancies, etc. This is why you need to ask about when it traded last and when the property was purchased last and how much they paid. If the seller bought it ten years ago for five million less than they're selling it

## [09:03 - 12:04]

for, their taxes again will be less than what you're paying. If you pay five million more, the taxes are going to probably be another ten thousand bucks a year. No problem. The tenants are paying for it anyway. Don't let it scare you. Just know. You know the old saying, hey, keep your friends close and your enemies closer. Know where they are. Also always ask the wrap up questions, which tend to surprise some brokers. Have you picked a buyer yet? Huh? Remember, I'm trying to get my LOI pushed through. Maybe there's three bidders. Maybe there's five bidders. I've been on deals with 50 different bidders. Have you guys picked a buyer? Do you have a favorite? Okay, does the seller have a favorite? Do you have a top three list? What would it take me to get on that list? Okay? You got to move yourself up on the list for them to pick you. Again, this is part of your due diligence. Where are you in the buying process? While you won't always get straight answers to these questions, it's good to ask and makes you seem like you're ready to move forward. I was working a deal yesterday, $190 million, and I could tell that these sellers had already picked another buyer. Even though they're saying, hey, we're still collecting all the bids. I was on the phone. I was actually listening to the call with my associate Ryan, and I could hear the broker on the other side not making a meeting. Couldn't find the time to do this. Okay? I grabbed the phone and said, hey man, have you guys picked a buyer already? No, no, no, we haven't. Do you have a favorite? No, no, no. Does the seller have a favorite? No, no, no. Hey, quit wasting my time. Okay? I sense that you guys already have a favorite. Is that true? Yes, it is. Good. I'm not wasting any more time on my research or my energy or my resources trying to run a deal down, and you guys have already picked a player. In this case, they had already done business with this other group on multiple deals. I'm new to them. He's like, Grant, you're not going to get the deal. But it was like pulling teeth to get the truth from them. That way I'm able to save my time, my money, and my resources, particularly my energy, and go look for other deals. Keep in mind. You're on the heels of your own personal due diligence. Do you want the deal? Have you shopped the neighborhood? Does it feel good? Does the data match up with your gut? Is this a right investment for you at this time? That's why you're doing the due diligence. At the end of the day, you, me, we are the marketplace. You don't need to go to the marketplace to determine what the marketplace is. You and I are the marketplace. That's why it's so critical. You know your market. You know what other people will pay. You know what your neighbor paid.

## [12:04 - 14:10]

You know what people will pay for a nicer kitchen or a better neighborhood or a safer environment. You know what they'll pay for a doggy park or a better playground. That's why you should be invested close to where you live. You need to know your market. You need to know your market. Your new mantra, I need to know my market. Yes, I know my market. Write it down a thousand times. In fact, once you just do this, know your market and you won't have to write it down. It'll be true. By the time you get to this point in the process, you need to know that this deal checks the boxes. Then map out a conversation with the broker based on your overview. You should also plan to talk about yourself and why you're the right buyer for the deal if it's not under contract yet. If there's competition, sell yourself. If you're new to the market, sell yourself hard because until you do that, until you get the deal, you're not going to be able to do the due diligence required that will cost you money and time. As you do more deals, you'll build more relationship with more brokers, brokers who can help you find deals and lock down deals. Remember, don't put a deal under contract that you don't plan on doing. Don't do due diligence if you're not sure you're going to do it. Otherwise, every relationship you have in that market will be wrecked. Soon enough, you'll be able to call a broker and say, hey, I love your deal at such and such address. Can you get me six more like it and he'll be bringing you deals. That's what happens at Cardone Capital now. We've done so many deals. I have people from Chicago, Houston, all across Texas, the Panhandle, Atlanta, Georgia, North Carolina, South Carolina. I got people in California calling me about deals right now because they know I'm real. They know we buy. They know we do everything we can to close. Understand the real due diligence starts before you even get a deal. Then you do the real due diligence.
